title: Approximating Iterated Multiplication of Stochastic Matrices in Small Space
speaker: Dean Doron
abstract: Matrix powering, and more generally iterated matrix multiplication, is a fundamental linear algebraic primitive with myriad applications in computer science. Of particular interest is the problem’s space  complexity as it constitutes the main route towards resolving the BPL vs. L problem.

proof_stages.label: IMM and space-bounded derandomization landscape
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates iterated multiplication of stochastic matrices, reviews probabilistic log-space computation and BPL versus L, and places exact and approximate matrix-product algorithms within the known space-complexity landscape.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic powering as the BPL-complete target and main bound
proof_stages.summary: Stochastic matrix powering is identified as complete for BPL-style derandomization, known bounds are compared, and the new nearly log n plus square-root-log n times log w space bound is stated.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic ingredients and Nisan as matrix-powering routine
proof_stages.summary: The proof is organized around Nisan's generator viewed as a randomized matrix-powering algorithm, the Saks-Zhou randomness-recycling framework, and Richardson iteration as an error-reduction tool.

proof_stages.label: Saks-Zhou recycling and shift-and-truncate bottlenecks
proof_stages.summary: Repeated applications of Nisan's routine raise powers through blocks, but reusing a seed creates adaptivity issues that shift-and-truncate resolves, after which the space costs of shifts and Nisan's precision requirements are isolated.

proof_stages.label: Low-precision Nisan plus Richardson error restoration
proof_stages.summary: The powering algorithm deliberately truncates matrices and asks Nisan only for mild accuracy, then uses Richardson iteration with access to the original matrix and mild approximations to recover high-precision approximations without paying log n space at every iteration.

proof_stages.label: Extension from powering to arbitrary products
proof_stages.summary: For distinct matrices the powering scheme is lifted to a product tree, with the main obstacle being the impossibility of independent shifts at all nodes; the talk sketches input-level shifting, a second Richardson-type correction, confidence amplification, and a related no-shift approach.

proof_stages.label: Black-box PRG formulation and open problem
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion recasts IMM approximation as the black-box problem of constructing small pseudorandom walk sets for read-once branching programs, explains why current PRGs lag behind algorithms, and poses making the Saks-Zhou-style constructions fully black-box as an open direction.

examples.name: Scalar rounding boundary in shift-and-truncate
examples.note: The decimal scalar value such as 1.5123, together with the near-boundary variant, illustrates why naive truncation may or may not hide dependence on the random seed and motivates adding a random shift before truncating.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker walks through concrete scalar outputs in shift-and-truncate, showing how truncation can erase seed dependence and how being near a rounding boundary makes two successful invocations truncate differently, motivating random shifts.

passes.reason: A scalar lying at or near a truncation grid boundary is a boundary case, and it is used to show naive truncation need not remove seed dependence.

A scalar lying at a truncation/rounding boundary is used to show truncation alone can fail to remove seed-dependence.

examples.name: Constant-width PRG status at widths three and four
examples.note: The contrast between recent black-box generators for width 3 and the lack of improvement over Nisan for width 4 illustrates the gap between algorithmic small-space approximations and fully black-box PRG constructions.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only states the known PRG status for constant widths—recent progress for width 3 and no improvement over Nisan for width 4—without analyzing or constructing either case.

passes.reason: Widths 3 and 4 are ordinary small constant-width branching-program parameters, cited to describe current PRG progress and an open gap rather than to refute a claim.

Widths 3 and 4 are ordinary small constant widths for branching programs/PRGs, cited to illustrate the current state of knowledge rather than to disprove a claim.
